text:
Imane Khelif's bout against Angela Carinihas gone down in Olympic historybecause of the controversy surrounding the bout, but there's more to this determined Algerian boxer than meets the eye.

Carini, her Italian opponent, withdrew from the round-of-16 match in Paris just 46 seconds in, claiming that Khelif had delivered the hardest punch she'd ever experienced. The 25-year-old could be heard telling her coach: "It's not right, it's not right."

Her emotional press conference ignited a worldwide controversy, with many questioning Khelif's gender and her eligibility to compete in women's events.

Khelif has found herself in the crosshairs of transgender sceptics like JK Rowling, who label her a 'biological man' and argue she shouldn't be allowed to compete.

However, reports suggest that Khelif is a biological woman with a rare condition known as differences in sex development (DSD), which can cause female individuals to have male chromosomes or anatomy.

Khelif, who was assigned female at birth and is registered as female on her Algerian passport, stated in a previously recorded interview ahead of the Paris 2024 Olympics that she "wanted to show the whole world what a brave woman Imane Khelif is".



The International Olympic Committee (IOC)has reaffirmed its stance on gender eligibilityfor the Paris Olympics, stating that athletes' gender is determined by their passports. In a statement released on Thursday, the IOC confirmed continuity with past Games practices.

Spokesman Mark Adams had previously clarified on Tuesday: "Everyone competing in the women's category is complying with the competition eligibility rules. They are women in their passports and it's stated that this is the case, that they are female."

In a defiant response to her disqualification from last year's World Boxing Championships, Khelif has spoken out. The International Boxing Association (IBA) disqualified Khelif after she failed a gender eligibility test, which the IBA insists was not related to testosterone levels but rather other competitive advantages.

Despite the IBA's confidentiality on the specifics of the test, President Umar Kremlev claimed that the tests indicated XY chromosomes, leading to Khelif's exclusion.



Reflecting on her challenging journey, Khelif - whose career record is 42 wins in 51 fights with nine losses - said: "The year 2023 was very difficult for me after a great year...

"It was a hard blow for me but I came back stronger to show my strength and my determination and show the whole world what a brave woman Imane Khelif is."

Speaking on Canal Algerie, she revealed: "I come from a conservative region and family. Boxing was a sport dedicated only to men. These are obstacles that I encountered when I started.

"I happened to sell bread in the street, I collected dishes and other objects to earn money and to be able to move around because I came from a very poor family."

Her passion for boxing ignited instantly upon entering the ring, and with unwavering resolve, she transitioned from her humble beginnings in a small village to the bustling city life and ultimately the capital.



Khelif regards her ascent as an "impressive story" and takes immense pride in her accomplishments. Reflecting on her unexpected rise in the boxing world, she said: "It was pure chance, I never imagined one day that I would become a boxer and that I would become world champion.

"I have always loved football and I played it in my little village. My father always preferred football to boxing. But I was very good at sports in my school and my teacher encouraged me to become a boxer since I had good physical qualities and he was right."

Khelif has her eyes set on Olympic glory, advancing to the women's welterweight quarterfinals this Saturday where she faces Hungary's Anna Luca Hamori. Despite Khelif's dominant win over her Italian adversary, underdog Hamori remains unfazed.

She boldly stated to the press: "I'm not scared. I don't care about the press story and social media. If she or he is a man, it will be a bigger victory for me if I win."



Yet, amid growing debates, Olympic champion Nicola Adamsraised concerns over fairness and safetyin women's competitions.

The two-time gold medallist expressed her views openly, saying: "After years of fighting for women's boxing to even exist in the Olympics and then all the training they go through to get there it was hard to watch another fighter be forced to give up on her Olympic dreams.

""People not born as biological women, that have been through male puberty, should not be able to compete in women's sport. Not only is this unfair it's dangerous!"

There promises to be significantly more discourse about Khelif the deeper she goes into the tournament. There will be some emotional scenes if she manages to go all the way in Paris.

title: Who is Imane Khelif? Inside the life of boxer at centre of Olympics gender row | Boxing | Sport | Express.co.uk
author: Julia Banim
description: Algerian boxer Imane Khelif has a fascinating story amid all the controversy surrounding her at the Paris Olympics.
